file_name: Bansal and Sundaramurthy - 2023 - Integrated convolutional neural network for skin cancer classification with hair and noise restorati.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses integrated convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for skin cancer classification. Specifically, it proposes Integrated Hair Removal (IHR) using image inpainting and Integrated Noise Removal (INR) using nonlocal mean denoising, combined with deep learning models (ResNet50, DenseNet121, ResNet152, VGG16, VGG19) to classify dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It presents original experimental work, including the creation of synthetic datasets with hair and noise artifacts, the proposal of integrated convolutional neural networks (IHR and INR) for artifact removal and classification, and the reporting of specific quantitative results (accuracy and loss metrics) comparing these methods against baseline models on the HAM10000 dataset.

title: Integrated convolutional neural network for skin cancer classification with hair and noise restoration
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of hair and noise artifacts in dermoscopic images that obscure skin lesions and reduce classification accuracy. The authors utilized the HAM10000 benchmark dataset and created synthetic Hair and Noise datasets to evaluate the impact of these artifacts on computer-aided diagnosis. They proposed Integrated Hair Removal (IHR) using image inpainting and Integrated Noise Removal (INR) using nonlocal mean denoising, integrated with deep learning models including ResNet50, DenseNet121, ResNet152, VGG16, and VGG19. The evaluation compared the performance of these integrated models against baseline classifiers on both clean and artifact-corrupted datasets. Results showed that IHR-DenseNet121, IHR-ResNet50, and IHR-ResNet152 achieved accuracy improvements of 2.3%, 1.78%, and 1.89% respectively over baselines in hair removal tasks. Similarly, INR-DenseNet121, INR-ResNet50, and INR-VGG19 achieved accuracy gains of 1.41%, 2.39%, and 18.4% respectively in noise removal tasks. The study concludes that removing hair and noise artifacts significantly improves the diagnostic accuracy of skin cancer classification systems.

all_task_types.target: Seven skin cancer classes (melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, melanoma, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'skin cancer classification' using dermoscopic images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (7 classes) using a final fully connected layer with softmax activation. The loss function used is categorical cross-entropy, which is standard for multiclass classification. The primary evaluation metric is classification accuracy.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (International Skin Imaging Collaboration / ISIC 2018)
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset of dermoscopic images of skin lesions, used as the ground truth source for the study.

use.training: Used as the ground truth dataset for training and validation of baseline and proposed models.
use.validation: Used for validation of baseline and proposed models.
use.testing: Used for testing baseline and proposed models.
use.other: Served as the source for generating synthetic datasets.

datasets_used.name: Hair Dataset (Synthetic)
datasets_used.description: A synthetic dataset created by adding hair strands to images from the HAM10000 dataset to simulate partial occlusion artifacts.

use.training: Used for training the Integrated Hair Removal (IHR) models.
use.validation: Used for validation of the IHR models.
use.testing: Used for testing the IHR models.
use.other: Created to evaluate the impact of hair artifacts on classifier performance.

datasets_used.name: Noise Dataset (Synthetic)
datasets_used.description: A synthetic dataset created by adding Gaussian noise (zero mean, scale σ varied from 1 to 30) to images from the HAM10000 dataset to simulate acquisition noise artifacts.

use.training: Used for training the Integrated Noise Removal (INR) models.
use.validation: Used for validation of the INR models.
use.testing: Used for testing the INR models.
use.other: Created to evaluate the impact of noise artifacts on classifier performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute accuracy and loss metrics without statistical significance testing.

results_obtained_in_each_dataset.extraction_error: Could not parse a balanced JSON object

architecture_used.main_model: Integrated Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) combining artifact removal (Integrated Hair Removal [IHR] with inpainting or Integrated Noise Removal [INR] with nonlocal means) and classification backbones (ResNet50, DenseNet121, ResNet152, VGG16, VGG19).
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning employed using pretrained weights obtained from training on the ImageNet dataset.

artifact_removal_methods.hair_removal: Integrated Hair Removal (IHR) using image inpainting (TELEA algorithm) and morphological operations (Gaussian blur, median blur, blackhat filter with 11x11 cross-shaped structuring element).
artifact_removal_methods.noise_removal: Integrated Noise Removal (INR) using nonlocal means denoising (OpenCV fastNlMeansDenoisingColored function) with luminance and color component parameters set to 10.

architecture_details.feature_extractor: Pretrained model weights loaded; final fully connected layer removed; remaining part used as feature extractor.

classification_or_output_head.modification: Final FC layer of pretrained models removed and replaced with a new FC layer.
classification_or_output_head.output_dimensions: [1 × 1 × N] where N is the number of classes (7)

preprocessing_techniques: Integrated Hair Removal (IHR) using image inpainting (TELEA algorithm) to restore pixels obscured by hair
Integrated Noise Removal (INR) using nonlocal means denoising to reduce noise artifacts
Gaussian blur (G_Blur) applied to the input image
Median blur (Med_blur) applied to the Gaussian-blurred image
Color space conversion from RGB to grayscale
Morphological operations including closing and blackhat filter (using an 11x11 cross-shaped structuring element) to extract hair contours
Thresholding operation to generate a binary mask for hair detection
Conversion of inpainted output from BGR to RGB color space

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Created by adding hair strands to images from the HAM10000 dataset to simulate partial occlusion artifacts. Total 5000 images were generated for this synthetic dataset.

Created by adding Gaussian noise with zero mean and a scale (σ) varied from 1 to 30 to images from the HAM10000 dataset to simulate acquisition noise artifacts. Total 5000 images were generated for this synthetic dataset.

conclusions: Hair and noise artifacts significantly obscure lesion pixels and reduce the classification accuracy of skin cancer diagnostic systems.
The proposed Integrated Hair Removal (IHR) using image inpainting and Integrated Noise Removal (INR) using nonlocal mean denoising effectively restores pixels affected by these artifacts.
IHR-DenseNet121, IHR-ResNet50, and IHR-ResNet152 achieved accuracy improvements of 2.3%, 1.78%, and 1.89% respectively over their baseline counterparts in hair removal tasks.
INR-DenseNet121, INR-ResNet50, and INR-VGG19 achieved accuracy improvements of 1.41%, 2.39%, and 18.4% respectively over their baseline counterparts in noise removal tasks.
The performance of the proposed integrated CNN approach surpasses that of existing methods for handling hair and noise artifacts in dermoscopic images.
The study acknowledges that skin lesion images also suffer from other artifacts such as poor color contrast, low illumination, moles, and bubbles, which remain challenges for accurate diagnosis.
Future work is identified as the development of deep learning methods for image inpainting and restoration to further address these limitations.
important_remarks: The study relies on synthetic datasets (Hair and Noise) generated by adding artifacts to the HAM10000 dataset rather than using real-world images with natural hair/noise occlusion, which limits the ecological validity of the findings.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance improvements were reported as absolute accuracy gains without significance testing.
The paper does not report external validation on independent datasets; evaluation was confined to the HAM10000 benchmark and its synthetic derivatives.

The proposed methods address only hair and Gaussian noise artifacts, explicitly acknowledging that other common artifacts (ruler marks, ink, bubbles, poor illumination, moles) remain unaddressed challenges.
The Noise Dataset used Gaussian noise with a scale (σ) varied from 1 to 30, which may not represent the full spectrum of real-world acquisition noise or sensor noise characteristics.

The study claims to be the first to use 5000 images adulterated with hair and noise, but the synthetic nature of this data means it does not reflect the distribution of artifacts in clinical practice.

While the paper mentions informed consent was obtained for the HAM10000 dataset, it does not provide specific details on the demographic distribution or skin type diversity of the HAM10000 subset used, which is a known limitation of this dataset regarding skin tone bias.
